publications.title: High-affinity oligoclonal TCRs define effective adoptive T cell therapy targeting mutant KRAS-G12D.
publications.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America
publications.authors: Sim MJW, Lu J, Spencer M, Hopkins F, Tran E, Rosenberg SA, Long EO, Sun PD.
publications.executive_summary: This study characterized four TCRs from a successful adoptive T cell therapy case targeting KRAS-G12D—a hotspot oncogenic mutation—in an HLA-C*08:02-restricted patient, where three TCRs (TCR9a, 9b, 9c) recognized a KRAS-G12D nonamer and one (TCR10) recognized a decamer peptide. Crystal structures revealed that the therapeutic TCRs achieved high nanomolar-to-low-micromolar affinities through an oligoclonal strategy: the three nonamer-specific TCRs shared conserved CDR2β and CDR3α contacts but varied CDR3β to achieve different affinities and HLA interactions, while TCR10 bound a distinct decamer conformation. The critical finding is that the G12D mutation creates a stabilizing salt bridge to HLA-C absent in wild-type peptides, enabling selective recognition of the neoantigen, and that TCR affinity inversely correlated with in vivo persistence—suggesting optimal affinity windows for therapeutic function. This work matters for neoantigen immunotherapy, particularly KRAS-directed cancer vaccines and T cell engineering, as it demonstrates how structural analysis of successful clinical responses can guide the design of high-affinity, polyspecific TCR therapeutics targeting common cancer-driving mutations.
